IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper addresses a practical and important problem—sentiment quantification for app reviews and tweets in Italian and English—and reports tools (App2Check, Tweet2Check) that outperform 19 state-of-the-art baselines on multiple benchmarks (including 1k and 10k app-review test sets and tweet corpora), which makes it immediately useful to practitioners. The provision of a web service and additional NLP outputs (topics/NER) increases practical adoption potential. However, withholding engine details, code and hyperparameters and relying on supervised models without disclosed implementation limits reproducibility and the ability of researchers to build on the work, so its impact is likely moderate rather than transformative. | B(3): The paper tackles a practical and relevant problem (sentiment quantification for app reviews and tweets) and reports outperforming 19 state-of-the-art tools on multiple Italian and English benchmarks, which suggests clear utility for practitioners and product teams. However, key implementation details are withheld (no engine details, code, or hyperparameters) and one tweet test lacks a neutral class, limiting reproducibility and diminishing potential influence on research methodology. The availability of a web service and additional NLP features (topic/NER) improves real-world adoption, but the opaque engine and absence of released artifacts make this contribution moderately impactful rather than field-changing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a practical and important problem—sentiment quantification for app reviews and tweets in Italian and English—and reports tools (App2Check, Tweet2Check) that outperform 19 state-of-the-art baselines on multiple benchmarks (including 1k and 10k app-review test sets and tweet corpora), which makes it immediately useful to practitioners. The provision of a web service and additional NLP outputs (topics/NER) increases practical adoption potential. However, withholding engine details, code and hyperparameters and relying on supervised models without disclosed implementation limits reproducibility and the ability of researchers to build on the work, so its impact is likely moderate rather than transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles a practical and relevant problem (sentiment quantification for app reviews and tweets) and reports outperforming 19 state-of-the-art tools on multiple Italian and English benchmarks, which suggests clear utility for practitioners and product teams. However, key implementation details are withheld (no engine details, code, or hyperparameters) and one tweet test lacks a neutral class, limiting reproducibility and diminishing potential influence on research methodology. The availability of a web service and additional NLP features (topic/NER) improves real-world adoption, but the opaque engine and absence of released artifacts make this contribution moderately impactful rather than field-changing.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): The paper describes supervised-learning tools specialized for app reviews and tweets in Italian and English, trained on standard datasets and evaluated against 19 state-of-the-art baselines. It maps outputs to a 1–5 sentiment scale and adds topic/NER steps, but no novel algorithms, model architectures, hyperparameters, or implementation details are disclosed. Given the well-studied nature of sentiment classification and the lack of methodological innovation beyond application/engineering choices, the contribution appears mostly a combination of known techniques with limited novelty. | B(3): The paper addresses a well-studied problem (sentiment analysis of tweets and app reviews) using supervised learning models tailored to two domains and two languages, which is largely incremental rather than a fundamentally new idea. Although the authors report strong empirical gains over 19 research baselines and evaluate on multiple sizable app-review and tweet benchmarks, the core approach (supervised classifiers producing 1–5 sentiment scores and additional NLP pipelines) appears to be an application/engineering effort rather than a novel methodological contribution. The lack of disclosed engine details or hyperparameters further limits evidence of a new technique, though the domain specialization and multilingual benchmarking provide moderate practical novelty.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper describes supervised-learning tools specialized for app reviews and tweets in Italian and English, trained on standard datasets and evaluated against 19 state-of-the-art baselines. It maps outputs to a 1–5 sentiment scale and adds topic/NER steps, but no novel algorithms, model architectures, hyperparameters, or implementation details are disclosed. Given the well-studied nature of sentiment classification and the lack of methodological innovation beyond application/engineering choices, the contribution appears mostly a combination of known techniques with limited novelty.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses a well-studied problem (sentiment analysis of tweets and app reviews) using supervised learning models tailored to two domains and two languages, which is largely incremental rather than a fundamentally new idea. Although the authors report strong empirical gains over 19 research baselines and evaluate on multiple sizable app-review and tweet benchmarks, the core approach (supervised classifiers producing 1–5 sentiment scores and additional NLP pipelines) appears to be an application/engineering effort rather than a novel methodological contribution. The lack of disclosed engine details or hyperparameters further limits evidence of a new technique, though the domain specialization and multilingual benchmarking provide moderate practical novelty.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper runs extensive comparative experiments across multiple held-out app-review and tweet test sets (Italian and English) and compares against 19 state-of-the-art tools (via iFeel) plus SentiStrengthIta, which supports the performance claims. However, there are no ablation studies or sensitivity analyses, and key engine implementation details and hyperparameters are withheld (non-disclosure), preventing reproducibility. Related work coverage is reported as adequate, and some limitations (annotation noise, withheld implementation) are acknowledged, but failure cases are not analyzed in depth. Overall, the empirical evaluation is solid in breadth but misses important analyses and reproducibility details. | B(3): The paper runs experiments on multiple sizeable test sets (Italian and English app reviews and tweets) and compares against 19 research baselines plus SentiStrengthIta, reporting MF1/Acc and per-class F1s, which supports the performance claims to a degree. However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported, and important reproducibility details are missing (engine implementation withheld, no hyperparameters, code not mentioned). The authors acknowledge some limitations (non-disclosure, annotation noise ~80% human agreement), but failure cases and deeper analyses are not provided, leaving important aspects unexamined.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper runs extensive comparative experiments across multiple held-out app-review and tweet test sets (Italian and English) and compares against 19 state-of-the-art tools (via iFeel) plus SentiStrengthIta, which supports the performance claims. However, there are no ablation studies or sensitivity analyses, and key engine implementation details and hyperparameters are withheld (non-disclosure), preventing reproducibility. Related work coverage is reported as adequate, and some limitations (annotation noise, withheld implementation) are acknowledged, but failure cases are not analyzed in depth. Overall, the empirical evaluation is solid in breadth but misses important analyses and reproducibility details.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper runs experiments on multiple sizeable test sets (Italian and English app reviews and tweets) and compares against 19 research baselines plus SentiStrengthIta, reporting MF1/Acc and per-class F1s, which supports the performance claims to a degree. However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported, and important reproducibility details are missing (engine implementation withheld, no hyperparameters, code not mentioned). The authors acknowledge some limitations (non-disclosure, annotation noise ~80% human agreement), but failure cases and deeper analyses are not provided, leaving important aspects unexamined.

REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): Critical training details and hyperparameters are explicitly not reported and the engine implementation is withheld due to non-disclosure, preventing exact reproduction. The data is only partially reproducible: some sources are named (e.g., Araújo et al. for Italian tweets) and test-set sizes are listed, but full datasets/access and preprocessing steps are not clearly provided. Code is not mentioned as available and model/configuration specifics are missing, though evaluation metrics (macro F1, accuracy, per-class F1) are specified. Together these omissions make faithful reproduction difficult. | B(2): Reproduction is difficult because key model and training details are missing: the paper explicitly does not report hyperparameters or internal engine implementation (non-disclosure), and no code is provided. The preprocessing/pipeline is only described at a high level (topic/NER applied) without operational details, and dataset availability is ambiguous (compositions are listed but not all sources/public access are confirmed). The evaluation metrics and procedures are well specified (macro F1, accuracy, per-class F1), which helps, but many crucial details required to reimplement or retrain the systems are absent.

reviewer_a.justification: Critical training details and hyperparameters are explicitly not reported and the engine implementation is withheld due to non-disclosure, preventing exact reproduction. The data is only partially reproducible: some sources are named (e.g., Araújo et al. for Italian tweets) and test-set sizes are listed, but full datasets/access and preprocessing steps are not clearly provided. Code is not mentioned as available and model/configuration specifics are missing, though evaluation metrics (macro F1, accuracy, per-class F1) are specified. Together these omissions make faithful reproduction difficult.

reviewer_b.justification: Reproduction is difficult because key model and training details are missing: the paper explicitly does not report hyperparameters or internal engine implementation (non-disclosure), and no code is provided. The preprocessing/pipeline is only described at a high level (topic/NER applied) without operational details, and dataset availability is ambiguous (compositions are listed but not all sources/public access are confirmed). The evaluation metrics and procedures are well specified (macro F1, accuracy, per-class F1), which helps, but many crucial details required to reimplement or retrain the systems are absent.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized and follows a logical flow (introduction, methodology, experiments, datasets, baselines, metrics, conclusion) and states its main contributions early (App2Check/Tweet2Check and their performance claims). The methodology, datasets, baselines and evaluation metrics are described clearly in the summary, supporting good overall presentation, but some methodological details are withheld (engine implementation and hyperparameters), which reduces transparency and clarity in the methods section. Writing quality is described as adequate, though the summary does not mention figures/tables or notation explicitly, suggesting minor presentation gaps that could be improved. | B(3): The paper is adequately organized and its main contributions and evaluation setup (datasets, baselines, metrics) are clearly reported early on. Writing quality and related-work coverage are described as adequate, and the experimental details (test sets, metrics, baselines) are presented. However, key methodological details are withheld (engine implementation not disclosed, no hyperparameters reported), which makes the methods section hard to follow and prevents full clarity on how results were obtained.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well-organized and follows a logical flow (introduction, methodology, experiments, datasets, baselines, metrics, conclusion) and states its main contributions early (App2Check/Tweet2Check and their performance claims). The methodology, datasets, baselines and evaluation metrics are described clearly in the summary, supporting good overall presentation, but some methodological details are withheld (engine implementation and hyperparameters), which reduces transparency and clarity in the methods section. Writing quality is described as adequate, though the summary does not mention figures/tables or notation explicitly, suggesting minor presentation gaps that could be improved.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is adequately organized and its main contributions and evaluation setup (datasets, baselines, metrics) are clearly reported early on. Writing quality and related-work coverage are described as adequate, and the experimental details (test sets, metrics, baselines) are presented. However, key methodological details are withheld (engine implementation not disclosed, no hyperparameters reported), which makes the methods section hard to follow and prevents full clarity on how results were obtained.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The topic (sentiment analysis of app reviews and tweets in Italian and English) and the empirical evaluation (multiple app/tweet test sets, comparisons to 19 state-of-the-art tools via iFeel, and standard metrics like macro-F1 and accuracy) are squarely within the scope and audience of top NLP/ML conferences. However, the paper appears largely applied/tool-oriented and withheld critical engine details (no implementation disclosure, no hyperparameters, code not mentioned), which limits reproducibility and technical contribution expected at top-tier venues. Given solid benchmarking but limited methodological transparency/novelty, the paper is borderline appropriate for a top conference. | B(3): The topic (sentiment analysis of app reviews and tweets in English and Italian) and the use of supervised models directly target the NLP/ML community. The paper evaluates on multiple sensible datasets (app reviews and publicly available tweet corpora) using standard metrics (macro F1, accuracy, per-class F1) and compares against many baselines (19 tools via iFeel, SentiStrengthIta), which aligns with venue expectations. However, the non-disclosure of engine implementation details, lack of reported hyperparameters and no mention of code release limit reproducibility and methodological transparency that top-tier NLP/ML conferences typically require, making the fit borderline.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic (sentiment analysis of app reviews and tweets in Italian and English) and the empirical evaluation (multiple app/tweet test sets, comparisons to 19 state-of-the-art tools via iFeel, and standard metrics like macro-F1 and accuracy) are squarely within the scope and audience of top NLP/ML conferences. However, the paper appears largely applied/tool-oriented and withheld critical engine details (no implementation disclosure, no hyperparameters, code not mentioned), which limits reproducibility and technical contribution expected at top-tier venues. Given solid benchmarking but limited methodological transparency/novelty, the paper is borderline appropriate for a top conference.

reviewer_b.justification: The topic (sentiment analysis of app reviews and tweets in English and Italian) and the use of supervised models directly target the NLP/ML community. The paper evaluates on multiple sensible datasets (app reviews and publicly available tweet corpora) using standard metrics (macro F1, accuracy, per-class F1) and compares against many baselines (19 tools via iFeel, SentiStrengthIta), which aligns with venue expectations. However, the non-disclosure of engine implementation details, lack of reported hyperparameters and no mention of code release limit reproducibility and methodological transparency that top-tier NLP/ML conferences typically require, making the fit borderline.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper presents empirical evidence (comparisons to 19 state-of-the-art baselines via the iFeel platform and SentiStrengthIta) across multiple test sets (A/B app review sets and tweet sets) using appropriate metrics (macro F1, accuracy, per-class F1), which supports the main performance claims. However, key technical details are withheld (engine implementation, model hyperparameters, and no code), the English tweet test lacks the neutral class, and no statistical testing or full dataset/training details are reported, which prevents independent verification and leaves some claims insufficiently supported. Overall the experimental comparisons are reasonably comprehensive, but reproducibility and some methodological details are missing, so the technical support is incomplete. | B(3): The paper’s main claims are supported by experiments comparing the proposed tools to 19 baselines (via iFeel) and SentiStrengthIta on multiple test sets (A-ita/A-eng, B-ita/B-eng) and two tweet datasets using MF1 and accuracy. However, important methodological gaps weaken the technical support: the engine implementation is withheld, no hyperparameters or code are reported, no statistical significance testing is mentioned, and the English tweet set lacks a neutral class. These reproducibility and evaluation-design concerns (and possible training/test overlap or imperfect baseline choice) justify a middle score: evidence is provided but not sufficiently rigorous to fully validate the strong “outperforms SOTA” claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents empirical evidence (comparisons to 19 state-of-the-art baselines via the iFeel platform and SentiStrengthIta) across multiple test sets (A/B app review sets and tweet sets) using appropriate metrics (macro F1, accuracy, per-class F1), which supports the main performance claims. However, key technical details are withheld (engine implementation, model hyperparameters, and no code), the English tweet test lacks the neutral class, and no statistical testing or full dataset/training details are reported, which prevents independent verification and leaves some claims insufficiently supported. Overall the experimental comparisons are reasonably comprehensive, but reproducibility and some methodological details are missing, so the technical support is incomplete.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are supported by experiments comparing the proposed tools to 19 baselines (via iFeel) and SentiStrengthIta on multiple test sets (A-ita/A-eng, B-ita/B-eng) and two tweet datasets using MF1 and accuracy. However, important methodological gaps weaken the technical support: the engine implementation is withheld, no hyperparameters or code are reported, no statistical significance testing is mentioned, and the English tweet set lacks a neutral class. These reproducibility and evaluation-design concerns (and possible training/test overlap or imperfect baseline choice) justify a middle score: evidence is provided but not sufficiently rigorous to fully validate the strong “outperforms SOTA” claims.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a large set of appropriate baselines (19 state-of-the-art tools via the iFeel platform plus SentiStrengthIta) across multiple English/Italian app-review and tweet benchmarks using standard metrics (MF1, accuracy, class F1), so the comparative evaluation is broadly comprehensive. However, fairness and interpretability are weakened because the authors withhold engine implementation details and report no hyperparameters, and the English tweet test lacks a neutral class which can distort class-wise comparisons. Related work coverage is described as adequate, but the summary does not indicate clear acknowledgement or deeper analysis when prior methods perform comparably, limiting the depth of the comparison. | B(4): The paper compares against a broad and relevant set of baselines (19 state-of-the-art research tools via the iFeel platform plus SentiStrengthIta) on multiple dedicated test sets (A-ita, A-eng, B-ita, B-eng, Italian and English tweets) using standard metrics (MF1, accuracy, per-class F1), so baseline coverage is strong. However, fairness and depth of analysis are limited because the authors withhold engine implementation and hyperparameters, the English tweet test lacks a neutral class (affecting direct comparability), and the summary does not indicate acknowledgment or detailed discussion when prior methods perform comparably — constraining clear analysis of differences despite adequate related-work coverage.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a large set of appropriate baselines (19 state-of-the-art tools via the iFeel platform plus SentiStrengthIta) across multiple English/Italian app-review and tweet benchmarks using standard metrics (MF1, accuracy, class F1), so the comparative evaluation is broadly comprehensive. However, fairness and interpretability are weakened because the authors withhold engine implementation details and report no hyperparameters, and the English tweet test lacks a neutral class which can distort class-wise comparisons. Related work coverage is described as adequate, but the summary does not indicate clear acknowledgement or deeper analysis when prior methods perform comparably, limiting the depth of the comparison.

However, fairness and depth of analysis are limited because the authors withhold engine implementation and hyperparameters, the English tweet test lacks a neutral class (affecting direct comparability), and the summary does not indicate acknowledgment or detailed discussion when prior methods perform comparably — constraining clear analysis of differences despite adequate related-work coverage.

main_claims: App2Check and Tweet2Check are supervised-learning-based tools that produce sentiment quantification on a 1–5 scale for sentences in Italian and English.
App2Check is specialized for app reviews and outperforms state-of-the-art research competitors on apps reviews benchmarks.
Tweet2Check is specialized for tweets and outperforms state-of-the-art research competitors on tweet benchmarks in both Italian and English.
The tools provide a web service and a user-friendly web application and apply additional NLP algorithms (e.g., topic/NER extraction) as part of their pipeline.
_summary.methodology: Two supervised learning predictive models were trained separately for app reviews and for tweets using a wide variety of language domains and user reviews; the tools output 1–5 sentiment scores mapped to negative/neutral/positive and apply additional NLP algorithms (topic/NER) to compute final answers, but engine details are withheld due to non-disclosure.
datasets: Test set A-ita (1000 Italian comments from Candy Crush Saga)
Test set A-eng (1000 English comments from Candy Crush Saga)
Test set B-ita (10,000 comments — 1,000 each from 10 popular apps: Angry Birds, Banco Posta, Facebook, Fruit Ninja, Gmail, Mobile Banking Unicredit, My Vodafone, PayPal, Twitter, Whatsapp)

Test set B-eng (10,000 comments — 1,000 each from 10 popular apps: Candy Crush Soda Saga, Chase Mobile, Clash Of Clans, Facebook Messenger, Gmail, Instagram, My Verizon, PayPal, Snapchat, Wells Fargo)
Italian tweets (3899 tweets from Araújo et al., 2016 / Sem 2016)
English tweets (1000 tweets randomly selected from the same source)
baselines: 19 state-of-the-art research tools run via the iFeel platform
limitations: Engine implementation details are not disclosed due to non-disclosure restrictions.
The English tweet dataset used does not contain the neutral class.
Human agreement on sentiment annotation is cited as ~80%, indicating inherent subjectivity/annotation noise in the reference labels.
No model hyperparameters or internal engine details are reported in the paper.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - Sentiment Analysis (app reviews and tweets)
